request.question: Emma Hamilton is an Austarlian-born actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art which is based in what city?

title: Eva Simatou
Antonia Kinlay
Estelle Kohler
Royal Academy of Dramatic Art
Katherine Kelly (actress)
Hugh Sinclair (actor)
Emma Hamilton (film)
Emma Hamilton (actress)
Tony Beckley
Tomahawk Theatre Company
sentences: Eva Simatou is a Greek actress.
She is a first class graduate of the Advanced Drama School Theatro Technis Karolos Koun (Athens Art Theatre).
She has also studied in London at City University and holds a BSc in Sociology and Media Studies and an MA in Arts Management & Criticism.
She was accepted by the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) and has successfully completed the Contemporary Drama programme.

She is a graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art In the West End, Kinlay has played Melody in Michael Longhurst's production of "Bad Jews" at the Theatre Royal Haymarket and Penny in "Three Lions" at the St James Theatre.
In 2016 she appeared in "The Suicide" at the National Theatre and played the title role in "Lady Anna: All at Sea" at the Park Theatre
Estelle Kohler (born 28 March 1940) is a British theatre and television actress.
Born in South Africa, Kohler made a name for herself as a Shakespearean actor in England.

She is a graduate of Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, of which she is an Associate Member, and was nominated a Laurence Olivier Award in 2000 for her performance as Paulina in the Barbican Centre's production of "The Winter's Tale" (1999 season).
She is also remembered for her performance as Hilary Nash (later Main) in the TV series "The Main Chance", opposite John Stride.
Her voice is featured in several audio books by Naxos Audiobooks.
The Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) is a drama school in London, England that provides training for film, television and theatre.
It is one of the oldest and most prestigious drama schools in the United Kingdom, founded in 1904 by Herbert Beerbohm Tree.
Katherine Kelly (born 19 November 1979) is an English actress.

A 2001 graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, she played Becky McDonald in the ITV soap opera "Coronation Street" from 2006 to 2012, for which she won Best Actress at the 2009 British Soap Awards and Best Serial Drama Performance at the 2012 National Television Awards.
Since 2013, she has starred as Mae, Lady Loxley in the ITV drama series "Mr Selfridge".
Hugh Sinclair (19 May 1903 – 29 December 1962) was a British actor born in London, England, the son of a clergyman.
He was educated at Charterhouse School and a graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.
His first marriage was to the actress Valerie Taylor.
In his book "The Stage Struck Me!"
fellow actor Neville Phillips felt Sinclair always played variations of himself, handsome, debonair, suave and witty and excelled in light comedy.
By contrast Phillips felt his wife, who Sinclair often appeared opposite, was a dramatic actress of tremendous power with a magnificent voice.
Emma Hamilton is a 1968 historical drama film directed by Christian-Jaque and starring Michèle Mercier, Richard Johnson and John Mills.
It was partly based on the 1864 novel "La Sanfelice" by Alexandre Dumas and depicts the love affair between Emma Hamilton and Horatio Nelson.
It was a co-production between Italy, West Germany, France and the United States.
Emma Hamilton (born 1984 in Melbourne) is an Australian-born British film, television and theatre actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.
Derek Anthony "Tony" Beckley (7 October 1929 – 19 April 1980) was an English character actor.

A graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art, Beckley went on to carve out a career on film and television throughout the 1960s and 1970s often playing villainous roles, as well as being a veteran of numerous stage productions.
Tomahawk is a theatre company based in Oxford, South East England.

Founded in 2005 by Alex Nicholls and Oliver Baird among others, experienced personnel work alongside young actors, directors and technicians, many of whom have gone on to train in London at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA), London Academy of Music and Dramatic Art (LAMDA) and Mountview Academy of Theatre Arts.

candidate.summarize1.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `passages`, produce the fields `summary`.

Role and goal: Summarize the first-hop evidence so the next agent can find the missing second-hop information. Produce a single short, factual `summary` that: (1) compresses the grounded facts in the provided passages; (2) exposes the bridge entity, the specific missing fact, or the retrieval direction needed for the next hop; and (3) gives concrete evidence anchors (quoted or clearly paraphrased phrases) that a retriever can use.

Content rules:
- Keep the summary concise and fact-focused. Do not attempt to produce the final answer; describe only what the passages support and what is still needed.
- Preserve the same passage facts and phrasing as you would otherwise (you may quote or paraphrase). Do not change which facts are exposed; only condense them.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from the passages to state a bridge or to justify a next-hop direction. Do not invent facts, infer new temporal facts, or treat composition as unsupported when the necessary premises are present.
- If the passages lack a required premise, or contain conflicting identity links, explicitly state which premise or identity link is missing or ambiguous so the next agent can retrieve or disambiguate it.

Date-format rule (narrow formatting change):
- When the summary includes a date token that appears in the passages (any calendar date or parenthetical date taken from the text), render that date using the canonical form FullMonthName day, year (for example, "September 27, 2017") inside the evidence description. This normalization only changes the string form of the date token; it must not add, infer, or imply any additional temporal facts, nor be used as a reason to produce a final answer.
- Otherwise, leave all other wording and evidence presentation unchanged. Preserve quotations or clear paraphrases of the same passage text; only adjust the date token's string format.

Output contract:
- Return exactly one field `summary` containing the compressed first-hop evidence, the bridge/missing-fact signal, and any retrieval direction needed for the next hop. Do not output final answers, extra metadata, or new fields.
candidate.create_query_hop2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, produce the fields `query`.

Produce exactly one compact retrieval query string (single line) in the `query` field and nothing else. The query should be a concise keyword-style search targeted to recover the missing bridge or supporting evidence needed by downstream agents.

General behavior:
- Use the visible facts in summary_1 as anchors (album titles, artist names, dates, relation terms) and compose a focused query that is likely to retrieve passages supporting the needed relation. Prefer concrete anchors and relation keywords over full sentences.
- Do not include explanatory text, user-facing prompts, or requests-for-clarification in the `query` field. Do not invent or assert missing facts (for example, do not supply an artist name that is not present in summary_1).
- Preserve the single-query output contract: emit exactly one string and no other fields or commentary.

Special-case for missing album artist or genre (narrow semantic boundary):
- If summary_1 shows an album title but does not include the album's artist or genre, or if summary_1 explicitly signals inability to determine genre or explicitly asks for the artist/passage (phrases such as "can't determine" or "please provide the artist"), then emit exactly one compact search phrase formatted as:
  <AlbumTitle> album artist genre
  Use only the visible album title as it appears in summary_1 followed by the three keywords: album artist genre. Do not add punctuation, explanatory words, alternate tokens, or candidate artist names.
- Do not apply this special-case if summary_1 already contains the artist or genre, or if the album anchor is an ambiguous short token that requires human clarification.

Fallback and downstream behavior:
- If retrieval using the emitted query returns no useful passages, the downstream agent may then ask the user for clarification or additional context. Do not ask the user inside the `query` field.
candidate.summarize2.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `context`, `passages`, produce the field `summary`.

Task: Integrate the first-hop `context` with the newly retrieved `passages` and produce a single concise `summary` that preserves the facts needed by the final answer. Resolve the earlier bridge when the supplied passages support doing so; retain uncertainty only when the post-retrieval evidence is genuinely insufficient, ambiguous, or conflicting.

Guidelines:
- Treat a concise answer in `context` as the primary claim. Do not replace that concise context answer unless the `passages` contain a direct, identity-aligned contradiction.
- Before attributing any fact from a passage to the entity or relation named in `context`, confirm identity alignment (clear aliasing or explicit linking). Do not assume two similarly named mentions are the same without such alignment.
- If the `passages` consistently corroborate the context claim and are identity-aligned, append one brief paraphrased supporting sentence that cites the corroboration (keep the summary compact).
- If the `passages` are weak, ambiguous, or conflict with each other or with the context claim (but do not provide an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction), preserve the context claim and append a single short clause that flags the uncertainty or contradiction. When flagging, you may optionally include a verbatim quoted phrase from a passage of up to 10 words to show the source wording. Do not invent resolutions beyond what the `passages` show.
- If the `passages` contain an explicit, identity-aligned contradiction to the context claim, state that the passages contradict the context claim and briefly note the conflicting passage fact (optionally with a ≤10-word quote). Only in this case may the summary stop asserting the context claim as definitive.
- Keep the `summary` compact (aim for 1–3 sentences). Use uncertainty language only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not default to uncertainty when composition across context and passages licenses a conclusion.
- You may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts from `context` and `passages` to form a licensed multi-hop conclusion; the supporting facts need not all appear in a single passage.
- Do not include long verbatim quotes, do not invent facts, and do not attempt to resolve contradictions beyond reporting them as present in the `passages`.

Output: a single `summary` string that follows the rules above.
candidate.final_answer.predict:
Given the fields `question`, `summary_1`, `summary_2`, produce the field `answer`.

Use only the information in the provided summaries as evidence. summary_2 is the later, integrated evidence state and should be the primary source for deciding the minimal answer; summary_1 may contain useful first-hop compression or retrieval-oriented language and may be used to supply premises that, together with summary_2, license a conclusion. Do not invent facts or use outside knowledge.

Emit only the minimal answer string supported by the visible summaries in the `answer` field. Do not include explanations, justification, or any additional text.

Preserve multi-hop composition: you may combine multiple explicit, identity-aligned facts across the summaries to form a licensed conclusion even when no single sentence states the whole relation. Distinguish licensed composition from unsupported inference; emit uncertainty only when a required premise, identity link, or relation is genuinely missing, ambiguous, or conflicting in the summaries.

Minimal-answer formatting rule (explicit):
- If the summaries jointly support one minimal literal and that literal appears verbatim in the summaries and consists of multiple contiguous tokens (for example: a full personal name, a full date, or a place name), emit that full literal exactly as it appears in the summaries (verbatim, unchanged). Do not truncate or coerce it to a single token.
- If the jointly supported minimal answer is itself a single-token label, emit exactly that single token (in lowercase) and nothing else.

When in doubt about whether the summaries license a single definitive literal, prefer to emit uncertainty only if the required supporting premise or identity link is actually missing, ambiguous, or conflicting. Do not make uncertainty the default when composition or transitive reasoning is required and the necessary premises are present.

Ensure the `answer` field contains only the minimal answer string supported by the summaries (subject to the formatting rule above).

response.hop1_query: Emma Hamilton is an Austarlian-born actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art which is based in what city?
hop1_docs: Emma Hamilton (actress) | Emma Hamilton (born 1984 in Melbourne) is an Australian-born British film, television and theatre actress and graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art.

Barbara Ewing | Barbara Ewing (born 1944) is a UK-based actress, playwright and novelist.  Born in New Zealand, she graduated from Victoria University of Wellington with a BA in English and Maori before moving to Britain in 1965 to train as an actress at RADA (the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art) in London.

Barbara Bryne | Barbara Bryne (born 1 April 1929) is a British-born U.S.-based stage, film and television actress.  Onstage she has appeared in comedy, dramatic and musical productions.  The London-born actress studied at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts (RADA).

Victoria Hamilton | Victoria Hamilton (born 1971) is an English actress.  After training at the London Academy of Music and Dramatic Art, Hamilton began her career in classical theatre, appearing in productions with the Royal Shakespeare Company and the National Theatre.  In 2002, she appeared in the London stage play "A Day in the Death of Joe Egg" alongside Clive Owen and later Eddie Izzard.
Lucy Davenport | Lucy Davenport is a British actress who graduated at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art in 1999.
Kirsty Besterman | Kirsty Besterman is a British actress of the stage and screen.  She trained at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art graduating in 2002.

Hannah Arterton | Hannah Jane Arterton (born 26 January 1989) is an English actress.  She was born in Gravesend, Kent.  Arterton attended Gravesend Grammar School for Girls and graduated from the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art in 2011.  Her feature film debut was the romantic musical "Walking on Sunshine" (2014).  She is the younger sister of actress Gemma Arterton.
response.summary_1: Passages show Emma Hamilton is a "graduate of the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art" (passage [1]); the bridge entity is the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA). To answer the question, confirm the city where RADA is based — passages include the anchor phrase "RADA (the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art) in London" (passage [2]); next-hop retrieval: consult the RADA (Royal Academy of Dramatic Art) page or an authoritative source to verify its base city.
response.hop2_query: Royal Academy of Dramatic Art location city
hop2_docs: Maastricht Academy of Dramatic Arts | The Maastricht Academy of Dramatic Arts (Dutch: "Toneelacademie Maastricht") is a college of dramatic arts located in the city of Maastricht in the Netherlands.  It is part of the Zuyd University (Hogeschool Zuyd).  The school is housed in a former Protestant orphanage in the historic Jekerkwartier in Maastricht.

Royal Academy of Dramatic Art | The Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) is a drama school in London, England that provides training for film, television and theatre.  It is one of the oldest and most prestigious drama schools in the United Kingdom, founded in 1904 by Herbert Beerbohm Tree.
American Musical and Dramatic Academy | The American Musical and Dramatic Academy (AMDA) is a college conservatory for the performing arts located in New York City and Los Angeles, California.

Tomahawk Theatre Company | Tomahawk is a theatre company based in Oxford, South East England.  Founded in 2005 by Alex Nicholls and Oliver Baird among others, experienced personnel work alongside young actors, directors and technicians, many of whom have gone on to train in London at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA), London Academy of Music and Dramatic Art (LAMDA) and Mountview Academy of Theatre Arts.

Stockholm Academy of Dramatic Arts | On January 1, 2011 the two academies Dramatiska institutet (the Dramatic Institute/University College of Film, Radio, Television and Theatre) and Teaterhögskolan i Stockholm (Swedish National Academy of Dramatic Arts/Stockholm Academy of Dramatic Arts) founded a new academy: Stockholms dramatiska högskola, Stockholm Academy of Dramatic Arts (SADA).

Mehdi Forough | Mehdi Forough (1911–2008) was an Iranian scholar, author, dramatist, writer on dramatic arts and culture, translator, and founder of the Academy of Dramatic Arts in Tehran.  A native of Esfahan, Mehdi Forough attended Danesh Sara-ye Aali in Tehran and went on to study at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Arts, London.  He did graduate studies at Columbia University, NYC, where he completed his thesis, "Comparative study of Abraham's sacrifice in Persian passion plays and Western mystery plays", 1954.  This work was later published by the Ministry of Culture, in Tehran.
Shereen Martineau | Martineau trained at the Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA).
response.summary_2: The Royal Academy of Dramatic Art (RADA) is based in London, England — passage [2] explicitly describes RADA as "a drama school in London, England."
